title: Whipped cocoa body butter
subtitle: An anhydrous body butter whipped from cocoa butter, shea butter, and sweet almond oil
excerpt: An anhydrous body butter whipped from melted cocoa butter, shea butter, and sweet almond oil. The cocoa contributes a faint chocolate aroma; whipping at the cool-down stage gives a light mousse texture rather than a solid slab. Spreads on dry skin and warms into a thin oil film.
sourceNotes: Anhydrous body butter ratios per Susan Miller Cavitch and standard cosmetic-chemist guides: hard butters 50-60%, soft butters 20-30%, carrier oils 15-25%. Whipping at 25-30 degrees Celsius (just-set stage) holds air for the mousse texture.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 250 ml jar (~220 g whipped butter)
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. The whipping step takes longer with a larger volume; allow 15 minutes of mixing rather than 5.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Anhydrous and shelf-stable. Best within 6 months for full whipped texture; the air will gradually settle out and the butter will firm up. A re-whip with a hand mixer restores the texture.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months stored cool and dark. The cocoa aroma fades over months; the butter is still useable. Discard if the jar develops a rancid smell or a yellow oily separation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cosmetic product made entirely of oil-soluble ingredients - no water, no aqueous extracts, no hydrosols. Anhydrous products don't support bacterial growth so they don't require a broad-spectrum preservative. Shelf life is limited by oil oxidation rather than microbial spoilage.

The temperature range at which a melted butter blend will hold air when whipped. For a cocoa/shea/oil blend this is 25-30 degrees Celsius - cooler than this and the butter is set solid; warmer and the air pumps out as fast as it goes in. Chill the bowl partially in cold water to hit the window.

content.text: A whipped body butter spreads more easily than a solid balm and feels less greasy on application even though the ingredients are identical. The
base (no water) means no preservative is needed; shelf life is the oxidation life of the oils, which the cool dark jar protects.

- the butter blend must be at 25-30 degrees Celsius when you start the mixer. Too warm and the air pumps straight out; too cool and the butter is already set. A cold-water bowl bath gets the temperature down quickly. The cocoa contributes a faint chocolate aroma that pairs with vanilla or sweet orange if you want to scent the batch; left unscented it smells of clean cocoa.

items.prepNote: Raw or refined cocoa butter. Raw retains the chocolate aroma; refined is neutral. Both whip identically.
Refined shea is smoothest for whipping; raw shea sometimes gives a grainy texture if the temperature isn't tightly controlled.
Carrier oil. Light, slow to oxidise.
Stabilises the blend; jojoba is very slow to go rancid and extends overall shelf life.
3-4% of total batch. Pairs with cocoa. Skip for a pure unscented butter.

content.text: Weigh the cocoa butter and shea butter into the metal bowl. Set over a
(saucepan with 3 cm simmering water). Stir until fully liquid - about 5 minutes. Remove from the heat. Add the sweet almond and jojoba oils. Stir to combine.

Set the bowl in a cold-water bath in the sink. Stir steadily; the temperature drops from melt (~50 degrees Celsius) to whipping window (25-30 degrees Celsius) in 10-15 minutes. The blend will start to cloud and thicken around 30 degrees Celsius - this is the right moment. The bowl should feel cool to the touch but the contents must still be pourable.

Stir in the sweet orange essential oil if using. Beat with the hand mixer on medium-high for 5-8 minutes; the blend pales from cream to ivory, fluffs up, and holds soft peaks. If it stays runny, the temperature was too warm - chill for another 2 minutes and re-whip. If it's grainy, the cocoa butter crystallised too fast - re-melt and start over.

Spoon the whipped butter into a clean dry 250 ml jam jar. Don't pack down; the air is the point. Lid and label with the date. The butter will firm up slightly in the first 24 hours; the texture stays light for 4-6 weeks before gradually settling.

Apply a small scoop to dry skin - elbows, knees, shins, hands. Warms from skin temperature to a thin oil film. Use clean dry fingers or a spoon; do not dip wet fingers into the jar (water in a water-free product needs a preservative the recipe doesn't include). Store at room temperature out of direct sun. 12 months shelf life. Discard if the smell goes rancid or oil separates as a yellow layer.
